criteria_results.title: Allegation 1: Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies cherry-picking as a distinct allegation category (Allegation Category 1). It describes the pattern of WCA, Whitmore, and Chao systematically allocating same-day profitable trades to favored accounts—principally Oceanic Fund I LP and Chao's personal brokerage account—while allocating unprofitable or less profitable trades to other client accounts including the Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund. The output includes specific statistics (68.4% profitable allocation rate for Oceanic Fund I LP vs. 41.2% for other accounts, 81.6% for Chao's personal account), the t-statistic (4.73, p<0.001), excess profits ($7.8M to Oceanic Fund I LP + $438K to Chao's account = $8.238M), and the statutory basis (Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act).

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Correct Statutory Provisions Cited
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly and repeatedly cites Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 in connection with the cherry-picking allegation. For example, it states: 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking," in violation of Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act.' Both statutory provisions are correctly identified as the basis for the cherry-picking allegation throughout the document.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: WCA Identified as Implicated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies WCA (Whitmore Capital Advisors LLC) as an implicated party in the cherry-picking allegation. For example, the output states: 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking," in violation of Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act.' WCA is explicitly named as a respondent throughout the document in connection with the cherry-picking scheme.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Whitmore Identified as Implicated (12% LP Interest)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies Whitmore as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation due to his 12% LP interest in Oceanic Fund I LP. Multiple sections of the output state that Whitmore holds a 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million, and that this personal financial interest created a motive for preferential trade allocation to that fund. The output includes statements such as 'The Division alleges that the favorable allocation to Oceanic Fund I LP was motivated by Whitmore's personal financial interest in that vehicle' and 'Whitmore holds a 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million as of December 31, 2023.' The Individual Exposure section also lists Whitmore with grounded figures including '12%' and '$22.2 million' in connection with the cherry-picking scheme.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Chao Identified as Implicated (Personal Account)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies Chao as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation due to her personal account receiving favorable allocations. Multiple sections explicitly state that Chao's personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) received 87 allocations from omnibus fills during the Review Period, of which 71 were same-day profitable (81.6% profitable allocation rate), substantially exceeding both the 41.2% rate for other client accounts and the 68.4% rate for Oceanic Fund I LP. The output explicitly states Chao is implicated in 'Allegation Category 1 (Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations, as the Head of Trading who was directly responsible for allocation decisions and who personally benefited from the cherry-picking pattern through favorable allocations to her personal account)' and quantifies her excess profits at $438,000.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Review Period January 1, 2021 â€“ December 31, 2023
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the cherry-picking review period multiple times. For example, it states 'Scope of Analysis This analysis covers all equity trades executed by Whitmore Capital Advisors LLC...during the period January 1, 2021 through December 31, 2023 (the "Review Period")' and 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking"' with the Review Period defined as January 1, 2021 through December 31, 2023.

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output includes all 5 of the required quantitative facts: (1) 4,217 equity trades analyzed - mentioned multiple times; (2) 1,843 profitable same-day trades - mentioned multiple times (e.g., 'Of the 4,217 trades analyzed, 1,843 were profitable on the day of allocation'); (3) Oceanic Fund I LP profitable allocation rate of 68.4% - mentioned extensively; (4) other client accounts rate of 41.2% - mentioned extensively; (5) t-statistic of 4.73 (p < 0.001) - mentioned multiple times. All 5 data points are clearly present in the output, satisfying the criterion of at least 3 of 5.

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states that Chao's personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) received 87 allocations from omnibus fills during the Review Period, of which 71 were same-day profitable, representing a profitable allocation rate of 81.6% (71 ÷ 87 = 81.6%). criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Chao's Personal Account 81.6% Profitable Rate
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states Chao's personal brokerage account received 87 allocations from omnibus fills, of which 71 were same-day profitable, representing a profitable allocation rate of 81.6% (71 ÷ 87 = 81.6%). criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the excess profits attributable to cherry-picking in favor of Oceanic Fund I LP totaled approximately $7,800,000 over the Review Period, with the breakdown: Calendar Year 2021: $3,100,000; Calendar Year 2022: $2,900,000; Calendar Year 2023: $1,800,000; Total (2021–2023): $7,800,000. criteria_results.title: Allegation 2: Misleading Form ADV Disclosures â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies misleading Form ADV Part 2A disclosures as a distinct allegation category. It explicitly references Item 6's materially false 'pro rata' allocation language ('no account or group of accounts receives preferential treatment'), Item 11's failure to disclose Whitmore's 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million, and Item 11's failure to disclose Chao's personal trading account at Meridian Clearing Corp. that received omnibus allocations. The output also references Item 12's failure to disclose the Lakeshore Trading arrangement. These are identified as violations of Section 207 of the Advisers Act and Rule 206(4)-1(a)(5), and the allegation is labeled as 'Allegation Category 2 (Misleading Form ADV Disclosures)' in multiple places throughout the output.

criteria_results.title: Form ADV: Filing Date March 28, 2023 Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly references the Form ADV Part 2A filing date of March 28, 2023 multiple times, including statements such as 'H&T reviewed the Firm's Form ADV Part 1A, Form ADV Part 2A (Brochure) most recently filed with the SEC on March 28, 2023' and 'WCA's Form ADV Part 2A (the "Brochure"), most recently filed with the Commission as an annual amendment on March 28, 2023 --- which was the most recent annual amendment filed during the Review Period.' The criterion is clearly satisfied.

criteria_results.title: Form ADV: False Pro Rata Allocation Statement in Item 6 Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the false statement in Item 6 of WCA's Form ADV Part 2A. It states: 'Item 6 of WCA's Brochure, which addresses the Firm's performance-based fees and side-by-side management arrangements, stated that WCA allocates trades on a "pro rata basis in proportion to each account's target allocation and available cash" and that "no account or group of accounts receives preferential treatment in the allocation of investment opportunities." This directly identifies the specific false pro rata allocation statement in Item 6 and characterizes it as materially false and misleading.

criteria_results.title: Form ADV: Omission in Item 11 Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies the omission in Item 11 of WCA's Form ADV Part 2A. (Account No. ending -7823) that received allocations from the Firm's omnibus trading account.' Both omissions are identified multiple times throughout the document with detailed analysis of their materiality and regulatory significance.

criteria_results.title: Allegation 3: Deficient Compliance Policies and Procedures â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies deficient written compliance policies and procedures as a distinct allegation category. It is labeled 'Allegation Category 3 (Deficient Written Policies and Procedures)' and referenced multiple times throughout the document, including in the statutory provisions section where Rule 206(4)-7 is cited as violated by this conduct, and in the WCA Compliance Program Deficiency section which extensively discusses the gaps in the Compliance Manual (Section 7.3 lacking omnibus allocation procedures, Section 9.1 lacking restrictions on employee omnibus allocations, etc.).

criteria_results.title: Deficient Compliance Policies: Rule 206(4)-7 Referenced
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output repeatedly and explicitly cites Rule 206(4)-7 under the Investment Advisers Act in connection with the deficient compliance policies allegation. For example, it states: 'Rule 206(4)-7 under the Advisers Act [17 C.F.R. § 275.206(4)-7] --- Requires every investment adviser registered with the Commission to adopt and implement written policies and procedures reasonably designed to prevent violation of the Advisers Act...' and 'The Division alleges that this rule was violated by the conduct described in Allegation Category 3 (Deficient Written Policies and Procedures).' The rule is cited numerous times throughout the document in direct connection with WCA's compliance policy deficiencies.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Section 7.3 Deficiency Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the Section 7.3 deficiency multiple times with specificity. For example: 'Section 7.3 of the Compliance Manual, titled "Trade Allocation," described a "rotation" methodology for the allocation of IPO (initial public offering) and secondary offering allocations among eligible client accounts. However, Section 7.3 contained no procedures whatsoever for the allocation of equity trades executed through omnibus accounts.' This is stated by section number and substance, clearly identifying that Section 7.3 only covered IPO/secondary offering rotation but lacked any procedures for omnibus equity trade allocation.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Section 9.1 Deficiency Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the Section 9.1 deficiency multiple times. For example: 'Section 9.1 of the Compliance Manual, titled "Personal Trading," required employees to pre-clear trades in individual securities and to report personal securities transactions on a quarterly basis and personal securities holdings on an annual basis, consistent with the requirements of Rule 204A-1 under the Advisers Act. However, Section 9.1 did not address or restrict employees from receiving allocations from the Firm's omnibus account in their personal brokerage accounts. This gap in the personal trading policy allowed Chao to receive 87 allocations from omnibus fills in her personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) during the Review Period without triggering any compliance review, pre-clearance requirement, or approval process.' This directly identifies both the pre-clearance requirement in Section 9.1 and the deficiency that it did not address omnibus allocations to personal accounts.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Omnibus Trading Volume Percentage Cited
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states multiple times that WCA executed 5,776 equity trades through the omnibus account out of a total of 7,912 equity trades during the Review Period, representing approximately 73% of total equity trading volume. criteria_results.title: Allegation 4: Failure to Maintain Books and Records â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies 'Allegation Category 4 — Failure to Maintain Required Books and Records' as a distinct allegation, and repeatedly cites both Rule 204-2(a)(3) and Rule 204-2(a)(7) under the Investment Advisers Act throughout the document. The output discusses WCA's failure to maintain contemporaneous allocation records for 45.2% of omnibus equity trades, the backdating of 312 Excel spreadsheets, and the requirements of both rules in detail.

criteria_results.title: Books and Records: Key Quantitative Facts
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output includes all three data points: (1) '2,614 of the 5,776 omnibus equity trades executed during the Review Period (2,614 ÷ 5,776 = 45.2%), WCA was unable to produce any written record' - mentioned multiple times; (2) '312 Microsoft Excel spreadsheets containing allocation records had metadata showing creation dates that post-dated the corresponding trade dates' - mentioned multiple times; (3) 'post-dated the corresponding trade dates by an average of seventeen (17) business days' - mentioned multiple times. All three quantitative facts are clearly present in the output, satisfying the criterion of at least 2 of 3.

criteria_results.title: Allegation 5: Bellini Directed Brokerage / Kickback Scheme â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies Bellini's directed brokerage scheme as a distinct allegation. It describes Bellini directing 23 block trades totaling approximately $48.7 million in notional value (approximately 9.4 million shares) to Lakeshore Trading LLC over an 18-month period (June 2022 through November 2023). It identifies Kevin Ostrowski as a 40% owner of Lakeshore Trading and Bellini's college roommate from the University of Virginia (1999-2003). The output also details the excess commissions (4.2 cents vs. 1.1 cents per share, totaling $291,400), the undisclosed personal compensation Bellini received ($92,600 in golf club fees paid by Ostrowski), and the disproportionate harm to the Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund ($187,000 of the $291,400 excess commissions). This is labeled as 'Allegation Category 5 (Breach of Fiduciary Duty / Directed Brokerage Arrangement)' and discussed extensively throughout the output.

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Commission Rate Disparity Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states 'Lakeshore Trading charged WCA's clients commissions averaging 4.2 cents per share on the 23 directed trades. By comparison, WCA's other executing brokers --- including Meridian Clearing Corp. and two other broker-dealers used by the Firm during the Review Period --- charged commissions averaging 1.1 cents per share for comparable equity block trades during the same period.' This appears multiple times in the output, satisfying the criterion.

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Excess Commissions Total Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the excess commissions paid to Lakeshore Trading on the 23 directed trades totaled approximately $291,400, calculated as approximately 9,400,000 shares × $0.031 excess commission per share = $291,400. This appears multiple times in the output, satisfying the criterion.

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Undisclosed Compensation from Ostrowski
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies Bellini's undisclosed compensation from Ostrowski, including the $45,000 initiation fee, monthly dues of $2,800 per month for 17 months totaling $47,600, and the total of $92,600 ($45,000 + $47,600 = $92,600). This appears in the 'Undisclosed Compensation to Bellini' section and is also referenced in the 'Individual Exposure' section with the grounded figures '$92,600; $45,000.00; $2,800.00; $45,000; $47,600'.

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund Identified as Victim
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund as the primary victim of Bellini's directed brokerage scheme. Multiple passages state that Bellini served as the portfolio manager for the Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund account and directed a significant portion of the Lakeshore Trading block trades from that account, resulting in the fund bearing approximately $187,000 (64%) of the $291,400 in excess commissions. criteria_results.title: Allegation 6: Obstruction / File Deletion â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies the obstruction/file deletion allegation as a distinct category. It describes Whitmore's instruction to IT Administrator Tomás Delgado to 'clean up the shared drive' by deleting trade allocation spreadsheets from 2021 and early 2022, occurring during the pendency of the SEC examination that commenced March 11, 2024. This is referenced multiple times throughout the output, including in the 'File Deletion Instruction' section, the 'Individual Exposure' section, and the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section, and is explicitly labeled as 'Allegation Category 6 (Obstructive Conduct During Examination)' with citation to Section 209(e) of the Advisers Act.

criteria_results.title: Obstruction: Section 209(e) Cited
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly cites Section 209(e) of the Investment Advisers Act multiple times as the statutory provision for the obstruction allegation. For example: 'Section 209(e) of the Advisers Act [15 U.S.C. § 80b-9(e)] --- Prohibits any person from obstructing or impeding the Commission in the conduct of any examination, investigation, or other proceeding under the Advisers Act. The Division alleges that this provision was violated by the conduct described in Allegation Category 6 (Obstructive Conduct During Examination), specifically with respect to Whitmore's instruction to delete trade allocation records from the Firm's shared network drive during the pendency of the SEC examination.'

criteria_results.title: Obstruction: 47 Deleted Files Recovered
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states: 'The Division's forensic analysis of the Firm's server, performed by the Commission's Office of Information Technology pursuant to a voluntary document production by WCA, recovered forty-seven (47) deleted files from the relevant folders on the Firm's shared network drive.' This directly satisfies the criterion that the output notes 47 deleted files were recovered by forensic analysis from the relevant folders.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_001: Trade Count Discrepancy (4,217 vs. 4,312)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not identify the discrepancy between the 4,217 trades referenced in the SEC enforcement referral notice and the 4,312 trades referenced in the statistical exhibit. While the output extensively discusses the 4,217 figure (mentioning it numerous times in the context of the OQR analysis) and briefly mentions '4,312 omnibus equity trades' in one passage under 'Scope of Analysis,' it never flags these two figures as conflicting or notes that the 95-trade difference is unexplained and could undermine the statistical analysis. The criterion specifically requires identification of this discrepancy as a potential issue with the statistical foundation of the cherry-picking allegation, which the agent failed to do.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_001: Source Documents Correctly Attributed
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not correctly attribute the 4,217 figure to the referral notice (sec-enforcement-referral-notice.docx) and the 4,312 figure to the statistical summary (sec-quantitative-analysis-summary.xlsx) as a noted discrepancy. While the output mentions both figures, it does not clearly distinguish their source documents or flag this as a discrepancy between the two documents. The 4,312 figure appears in the output attributed to the 'Scope of Analysis' section (which corresponds to the quantitative analysis summary), but the output does not explicitly call out that 4,217 comes from the referral notice and 4,312 comes from the statistical summary, nor does it flag this as a discrepancy between source documents in the Discrepancies and Defense Issues section.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_002: Criminal Obstruction Exposure Flagged (18 U.S.C. Â§Â§ 1505/1519)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly flags criminal exposure under both 18 U.S.C. § 1519 and 18 U.S.C. § 1505 in the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section. It states: 'The alleged obstruction / records destruction carries PARALLEL CRIMINAL exposure beyond the civil counts: 18 U.S.C. § 1519 (destroying, altering, or falsifying records with intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within federal agency jurisdiction --- a felony carrying up to 20 years...) and 18 U.S.C. § 1505 (corruptly obstructing a pending federal agency proceeding, such as an SEC examination --- up to 5 years). SEC Enforcement routinely refers obstruction and spoliation conduct to DOJ under parallel-proceedings practice; counsel should assume a criminal referral is possible, evaluate Fifth Amendment implications for any individual testimony, and coordinate the Wells / examination response accordingly.' This directly satisfies the criterion.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_003: Bellini Compensation Calculation Gap ($92,600 vs. $103,800)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not identify the discrepancy between the bank records showing 21 months of country club payments (May 2022 through January 2024) at $2,800/month = $58,800 in dues plus $45,000 initiation = $103,800 total, versus the referral notice's calculation of 17 months of dues ($47,600) plus $45,000 = $92,600. The output mentions the $92,600 figure and the 17-month calculation from the referral notice, and it references the bank records exhibit showing entries like 'Total Months of Dues: 21,' but it never connects these to identify the $11,200 discrepancy between what the bank records show (21 months × $2,800 = $58,800 + $45,000 = $103,800) and what the referral notice calculates ($92,600). The criterion specifically requires identifying this calculation gap, which the agent failed to do.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_003: Defense Significance of Compensation Gap Noted
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not discuss the $92,600 vs. $103,800 compensation discrepancy at all. The output references the $92,600 figure (calculated as $45,000 initiation fee + $47,600 in monthly dues) and the $8,622,000 total ill-gotten gains figure, but never identifies or discusses any discrepancy between $92,600 and $103,800. There is no mention of $103,800 anywhere in the output, and consequently no discussion of implications such as whether the SEC is understating total undisclosed compensation, whether some months had different amounts, how this affects disgorgement calculations, or whether the inconsistency undermines the reliability of the SEC's financial analysis.

criteria_results.title: Scienter Distinction Between Section 206(1) and 206(2) Flagged
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the scienter distinction between Section 206(1) and Section 206(2) in the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section, stating: 'Section 206(2) requires only NEGLIGENCE, not scienter --- a lower bar than Section 206(1). The same conduct may sustain a 206(2) claim even where intent for 206(1) cannot be established.' This directly flags the distinction as a defense issue, noting that the same conduct could sustain a 206(2) claim even where intent for 206(1) cannot be established, which is precisely the defense strategy relevance the criterion requires.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_005: Form ADV Disclosure Obligations for Whitmore's LP Interest Analyzed Beyond Restating Allegation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output repeatedly restates the allegation that Whitmore's 12% LP interest in Oceanic Fund I LP was not disclosed in Item 11 of the Form ADV Part 2A, and references Rule 206(4)-1 and Rule 206(4)-2 generically. However, it never identifies any of the three specific disclosure obligations required by the criterion: (a) Item 11.A requiring disclosure of proprietary interests or conflicts, (b) Item 6 requiring disclosure of conflicts from principal investing alongside clients in a pooled vehicle with allocation discretion, or (c) SEC Staff guidance or Risk Alerts identifying undisclosed principal investments in pooled vehicles as a high-priority enforcement area. The output merely restates the allegation and cites general provisions without analyzing the specific applicable Form ADV disclosure requirements.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_006: Statute of Limitations Concern Under 28 U.S.C. Â§ 2462
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly addresses the statute of limitations under 28 U.S.C. § 2462 in the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section. It notes that SEC civil monetary penalties are subject to the five-year statute of limitations under 28 U.S.C. § 2462, and specifically analyzes conduct dated to 2021 (cherry-picking scheme and Chao omnibus allocations), stating that the five-year civil-penalty window for 2021 conduct closes around the end of 2026. The output also distinguishes between civil penalties and disgorgement, noting that disgorgement runs on a separate clock under 15 U.S.C. § 78u(d)(8) (NDAA 2021) allowing ten years for scienter-based violations. While the output does not specifically cite Kokesh v. SEC, it does identify the core statute of limitations concern for early Review Period (2021) conduct under 28 U.S.C. § 2462, which satisfies the criterion's requirement to note that conduct from early 2021 may be approaching or past the five-year limitations period for civil penalties.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_006: Specific 2021 Conduct Identified as Potentially Time-Barred
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the cherry-picking scheme conduct dated to 2021 as potentially subject to statute of limitations concerns, noting that 'the record dates this conduct to 2021; under 28 U.S.C. § 2462 the five-year civil-penalty window for it closes around the end of 2026.' Similarly, the Chao Omnibus Allocations to Personal Account conduct dated to 2021 is flagged with the same analysis. The output also specifically references the forensic recovery of 'trade allocation spreadsheets for the period from January 2021 through approximately March 2022' and discusses the discrepancy between 'January 2021' and 'March 2022' as the file deletion period start year, noting this '14-month discrepancy (Jan 2021 vs Mar 2022) could materially alter the charged period, statute of limitations calculations.' This goes beyond abstract discussion of limitations periods and specifically ties early 2021 cherry-picking conduct to the limitations analysis.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_007: Date Discrepancy in Obstruction Allegation (April 15 vs. April 22)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not identify the discrepancy between the April 22, 2024 date mentioned in the referral notice for Whitmore's file deletion instruction and the April 15, 2024 date on the actual email exhibit (whitmore-delgado-email.eml). The output references the April 22, 2024 date multiple times and notes the email to Delgado as Exhibit A, but never flags the one-week discrepancy between the narrative date (April 22) and the email's actual date (April 15). The discrepancies section lists other date conflicts but does not include this specific issue.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_007: Defense Significance of Date Discrepancy Discussed
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses the April 22, 2024 deletion instruction extensively and notes that the language 'clean up the shared drive' could be read innocently as routine IT maintenance. However, the criterion specifically asks about the defense significance of an 'April 15 vs. April 22 date discrepancy.' The agent's output never identifies or discusses any April 15 date or a discrepancy between April 15 and April 22. The output only references April 22 as the date of the deletion instruction. criteria_results.title: ISSUE_008: Chao's Undisclosed Brokerage Account as Separate Violation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively discusses Chao's personal brokerage account (ending -7823) at Meridian Clearing Corp. and its receipt of 87 omnibus allocations, and notes that Item 11 of the Form ADV failed to disclose this account. However, the output never identifies the specific issue that Chao's Meridian Clearing account (ending -7823) does not appear on WCA's employee personal securities transaction report log (wca-employee-trading-log.xlsx), which lists only a Hartleigh IRA (ending -4401) for Chao. The criterion requires identifying this as a distinct compliance failure — a violation of Rule 204A-1 requiring disclosure of personal brokerage accounts — separate from the cherry-picking allegation. The agent does not reference the employee trading log discrepancy or frame the undisclosed account as a standalone Rule 204A-1 violation based on failure to disclose the account to the firm itself (as opposed to clients via Form ADV).

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_008: Rule 204A-1 (Code of Ethics) Referenced
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly references Rule 204A-1 multiple times. For example, it states that WCA 'has adopted a Code of Ethics (the "Code") pursuant to Rule 204A-1 under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940' and discusses Section 9.1's personal trading requirements as being 'consistent with the requirements of Rule 204A-1 under the Advisers Act.' The output also notes that the Compliance Manual's failure to address employee receipt of omnibus allocations in personal accounts created a gap separate from the direct cherry-picking violation, and explicitly connects this to Rule 204A-1 obligations. The output further states that 'Rule 204A-1 requires a written code of ethics with personal securities transaction and holdings reporting' and discusses whether WCA's code satisfies these mandates, while noting that restrictions on personal account allocations from omnibus fills may be required under separate regulatory provisions. This satisfies the criterion of referencing Rule 204A-1 and connecting the undisclosed employee brokerage account to a specific regulatory requirement.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_009: Omnibus Trade Percentage Discrepancy (73% vs. 78%)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the discrepancy between the 73% figure (from the SEC enforcement referral notice: 5,776/7,912 trades) and the 78% figure (from the statistical exhibit). This appears in multiple places in the output, including in the 'Section 7.3 Trade Allocation Procedures' section where it states 'These 5,776 omnibus trades represented approximately 78% of WCA's total equity trading volume during the Review Period' (from the quantitative analysis) alongside the 73% figure from the referral notice. The output also notes in the 'Chao Omnibus Allocations to Personal Account' section: 'The controlling source requires that the annual compliance review test trade allocation patterns given the firm's heavy reliance on omnibus trading (approximately 78% of equity trading volume)' while elsewhere consistently citing 73% from the referral notice. The discrepancy between 73% (5,776/7,912) stated in the referral notice and 78% stated in the statistical exhibit is thus identified in the output.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_009: Explanation of Why Omnibus Percentage Discrepancy Matters
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not provide any concrete explanation for why the 73% vs. 78% discrepancy in omnibus trading percentage matters. While the output repeatedly references both figures (73% in some places, 78% in others), it never discusses the implications of this inconsistency. There is no explanation that the discrepancy might stem from different denominators or datasets, no suggestion that it could indicate unreliable underlying data in the SEC's analysis, and no discussion of how the defense could use it to challenge the statistical methodology. The figures simply appear in different parts of the document without any analysis of why the inconsistency is significant.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_010: WCA's Respondeat Superior / Failure to Supervise Liability
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not address WCA's respondeat superior or control person liability specifically for Bellini's directed brokerage conduct, nor does it identify potential 'failure to supervise' exposure under Section 203(e)(6) of the Investment Advisers Act. While the output names WCA as a respondent and discusses Bellini's conduct extensively, it does not analyze WCA's entity-level liability theory based on Bellini's actions through respondeat superior principles or failure to supervise. The output discusses WCA's compliance failures generally (Rule 206(4)-7 violations, inadequate compliance manual, etc.) but never specifically frames WCA's liability for Bellini's directed brokerage scheme in terms of supervisory failure or respondeat superior doctrine. The criterion requires the output to flag that WCA's failure to detect the Lakeshore Trading arrangement through its compliance and best execution review processes supports a failure-to-supervise charge under Section 203(e)(6), which is entirely absent from the output.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Cherry-Picking Allegation (12% LP Interest)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively addresses Whitmore's personal exposure to the cherry-picking allegation due to his 12% LP interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million. Multiple sections explicitly identify this interest as creating financial motive for preferential trade allocation, including statements such as 'Whitmore holds a 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million as of December 31, 2023. Superior investment performance in Oceanic Fund I LP directly increases the value of Whitmore's limited partnership interest' and 'The Division alleges that the favorable allocation to Oceanic Fund I LP was motivated by Whitmore's personal financial interest in that vehicle.' The output also discusses his dual financial interests (12% LP interest plus 62% WCA ownership receiving management fees and performance allocations) as creating compounded incentives for cherry-picking, and identifies him as individually exposed to enforcement action.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Form ADV Misrepresentation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly addresses Whitmore's personal exposure to the Form ADV misrepresentation allegation in multiple places. For example, it states: 'The Division alleges that Whitmore, as CEO, majority owner, and the individual with ultimate responsibility for the Firm's regulatory filings, and Chao, as a managing director with direct knowledge of the allocation practices and the existence of her personal trading account, were responsible for ensuring the accuracy and completeness of the Firm's Form ADV disclosures. Their failure to ensure accurate and complete disclosures constitutes a violation of Section 207 of the Advisers Act.' It also notes Whitmore's failure to disclose his 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP (valued at ~$22.2 million) in Item 11 of the Brochure as a material omission, and identifies Section 207 violations tied directly to Whitmore's role and responsibilities. The output further identifies Whitmore as bearing 'primary responsibility for the adequacy of the Firm's compliance policies and procedures' and notes his personal liability exposure for Form ADV disclosure violations.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Obstruction Allegation (File Deletion)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively addresses Whitmore's personal exposure to the obstruction allegation. It identifies that on or about April 22, 2024, during the pendency of the SEC examination, Whitmore instructed IT Administrator Tomás Delgado to 'clean up the shared drive' by deleting trade allocation spreadsheets from 2021 and early 2022. The output notes this occurred while document requests were outstanding, discusses Section 209(e) of the Advisers Act as the statutory basis for obstruction charges, analyzes the timing relative to the examination commencement (March 11, 2024), and discusses the inference of willful obstruction. It also flags parallel criminal exposure under 18 U.S.C. § 1519 and § 1505, and identifies Whitmore personally as the individual responsible for the deletion instruction. The output clearly identifies Whitmore's personal obstruction exposure throughout multiple sections.

criteria_results.title: Individuals at Risk: Chao's Personal Exposure Summarized
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively addresses Chao's personal exposure. It identifies her 81.6% profitable allocation rate (71 of 87 allocations), her personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) at Meridian Clearing Corp., the $438,000 in excess profits allocated to her personal account, her role as Head of Trading with day-to-day discretion over omnibus allocations, her implication in the cherry-picking allegation under Sections 206(1) and 206(2), the undisclosed personal brokerage account receiving omnibus allocations (Form ADV Item 11 omission), and her exposure to individual sanctions including industry bars, civil penalties, and disgorgement. The output also breaks down her year-by-year excess profits ($162K, $189K, $87K) and notes she is implicated in four of six allegation categories.

criteria_results.title: Individuals at Risk: Bellini's Personal Exposure Summarized
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively addresses Bellini's personal exposure. It identifies his role as Vice President and Portfolio Manager, his direction of 23 block trades totaling $48.7 million to Lakeshore Trading LLC, his personal relationship with Kevin Ostrowski (college roommates at University of Virginia, 1999-2003, who holds 40% ownership of Lakeshore Trading), the excess commissions of $291,400 (4.2 cents vs. 1.1 cents per share), and the undisclosed personal compensation of $92,600 ($45,000 initiation fee + $47,600 in monthly dues at Bayshore Palms Golf & Country Club paid by Ostrowski on Bellini's behalf). The output also notes Bellini's potential individual sanctions including industry bar, civil monetary penalties, disgorgement, and injunctive relief, and identifies him as implicated in Allegation Category 5 (Breach of Fiduciary Duty / Directed Brokerage Arrangement).

criteria_results.title: Total Alleged Ill-Gotten Gains / Client Harm Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the total alleged ill-gotten gains figure. In the 'Individual Exposure' section under Marcus T. Bellini, the output states: 'The SEC quantifies total alleged ill-gotten gains and client harm at $8,622,000, comprising $8,238,000 in cherry-picking excess profits (split $7.8M to Oceanic Fund I LP and $438K to Chao's personal account), $291,400 in excess commissions to Lakeshore Trading, and $92,600 in undisclosed compensation to Bellini.' This matches the criterion's required figure of approximately $8.622 million with the correct breakdown across all three allegation categories.

criteria_results.title: Hargrove & Tilton Review: Failure to Test Trade Allocation Noted
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively and repeatedly notes that the Hargrove & Tilton annual compliance review (dated December 8, 2023) did not test trade allocation patterns despite WCA's heavy reliance on omnibus trading. This is identified as a critical deficiency in WCA's compliance infrastructure throughout the document, including explicit statements such as 'The annual compliance review conducted by Hargrove & Tilton LLP for the calendar year 2023, dated December 8, 2023, did not test trade allocation patterns despite the Firm's heavy reliance on omnibus trading' and multiple OMISSION/DEVIATION findings flagging this gap as critical severity. The output clearly identifies this omission as relevant evidence of WCA's deficient compliance infrastructure.

criteria_results.title: Key Dates: Examination Timeline Accurately Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output correctly states all five key dates: (1) examination commenced March 11, 2024 (stated multiple times); (2) deficiency letter issued August 2, 2024 (stated multiple times); (3) referral to Enforcement on October 18, 2024 (stated multiple times); (4) referral notice dated November 6, 2024 (stated in the discrepancies section); and (5) served November 12, 2024 (stated as 'Based on a service date of November 12, 2024'). All five dates are accurately stated, well exceeding the minimum threshold of three.

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states multiple times that Whitmore holds a 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million, calculated as 12% of $185 million in net assets. This correctly states the value and the calculation.

criteria_results.title: Email Exhibit Language Quoted or Paraphrased
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively references the Whitmore-to-Delgado email and quotes its language multiple times. For example, it quotes 'clean up the shared drive' and 'clean up of legacy files on the shared drive' and 'remove the old allocation folders from Q1 2021 through Q1 2022.' The output also explicitly notes the ambiguous/innocent interpretation, stating the language 'is consistent with routine IT maintenance practices' and 'is also susceptible to innocent interpretations involving routine data management or archival practices,' and in the defense issues section it steelmans the innocent reading that the instruction can be read as 'routine file/records housekeeping or ordinary-course IT cleanup.'
